transcript_events.text: How is is going?
Sent 1 queued message to the provider.
I’m checking the live workflow, the two queued goals, runtime health, and repository state so I can separate real progress from stale supervisor events.

The repository itself is healthy and synchronized, but the live evidence has one inconsistency: both goals say “in progress” while the process summary reports zero implementation agents. I’m following that path now to tell whether work completed, stalled, or was orphaned during the runtime transition.

It’s going well. Live verification shows:

- Two Codex implementation agents are actively running.
- Jira export is being converted from per-Goal to bulk selected-Goals export; substantial code is written and tests are compiling/running.
- Goal Follow Logs has its UX contract settled—tail/head ordering, live search, SSE updates, and safe link formatting—and implementation is underway.
- Refine’s daemon and workflow runners are reachable.
- `main` is clean and matches `origin/main`; earlier completed work is merged.
- The earlier provider launch and state-sync failures have recovered. No current authentication or provider failure is visible.

Nothing needs your decision at the moment. The next meaningful checkpoint is when these agents commit their implementations and enter validation/review.

It’s going well. Live verification shows:

- Two Codex implementation agents are actively running.
- Jira export is being converted from per-Goal to bulk selected-Goals export; substantial code is written and tests are compiling/running.
- Goal Follow Logs has its UX contract settled—tail/head ordering, live search, SSE updates, and safe link formatting—and implementation is underway.
- Refine’s daemon and workflow runners are reachable.
- `main` is clean and matches `origin/main`; earlier completed work is merged.
- The earlier provider launch and state-sync failures have recovered. No current authentication or provider failure is visible.

Nothing needs your decision at the moment. The next meaningful checkpoint is when these agents commit their implementations and enter validation/review.
Provider turn completed.
Do Goal rounds now have context from previous rounds?
Sent 1 queued message to the provider.
I’ll trace the Goal-round context assembly in the current Refine code and tests, then answer from the implementation rather than the event summary alone.
The active implementation path does now carry prior-round history. I’m checking the exact boundary—what is included, what is deliberately omitted, and whether it is covered by a regression test.

Yes. Current `main` builds each Goal-agent prompt with:

- Goal-level context and notes.
- Every earlier Round’s prompt, implementation report, governance, and quality results.
- The latest Round as the authoritative instruction when context conflicts.

Raw Round logs are deliberately excluded, so this is structured history rather than a full transcript. See [workflow/mod.rs](/home/buddy/projects/refine-next/src/workflow/mod.rs:1591) and [goal-agent.md](/home/buddy/projects/refine-next/src/prompts/goal-agent.md:1).

Verified with the focused regression test: **1 passed, 0 failed**.

Yes. Current `main` builds each Goal-agent prompt with:

- Goal-level context and notes.
- Every earlier Round’s prompt, implementation report, governance, and quality results.
- The latest Round as the authoritative instruction when context conflicts.

Raw Round logs are deliberately excluded, so this is structured history rather than a full transcript. See [workflow/mod.rs](/home/buddy/projects/refine-next/src/workflow/mod.rs:1591) and [goal-agent.md](/home/buddy/projects/refine-next/src/prompts/goal-agent.md:1).

Verified with the focused regression test: **1 passed, 0 failed**.
Provider turn completed.
Sent 1 queued message to the provider.

I’m taking over the queued supervisor turn and will track the workflow map against live process, Git, projection, operation, and activity evidence until the queue is genuinely idle. I’ll preserve the existing merge/worktree and provider boundaries, and I’ll surface any consequential choice rather than guessing.

The first blind spot is already clear: the workflow map says one `todo`, but there is no live workflow-agent Goal ID, while a supervisor chat operation is running. I’m checking the goal record, durable operations, runner output, and both relevant Git checkouts before deciding whether this is normal dispatch latency or lost work.

The queued Goal was not lost: it moved to `in-progress`, has an isolated worktree, and a live Codex implementation process is attached to the recorded execution ID. Git is clean at the start, the projection contains the same state transition, and the daemon/workflow workers are healthy. I’m now following the implementation transcript and branch changes rather than intervening in a healthy run.

The implementation process is active and still in repository mapping—specifically tracing the existing Feature Goal ordering, dependency, create, and edit paths. No files or commits have changed yet, so there is no partial implementation to recover or duplicate. I’m keeping the workflow-owned agent as the sole implementer and watching for forward progress or a provider/tool failure.

The agent has reached a sound architectural choice: dependency intent will reuse Refine’s durable ordered-Goal model instead of creating a parallel dependency store. The provider process is alive but currently in a long reasoning interval with no filesystem writes; that is not yet a stall, and I’m watching its transcript/CPU and the durable execution record for a timeout or provider failure.

Implementation is now materialized in seven scoped files: a new inline Feature-Goal interaction module, Feature detail wiring, responsive styling, intent documentation, and browser tests. Initial JavaScript syntax checks, the targeted new browser test, and diff hygiene all passed; the agent is reviewing responsive/error-state details before broader targeted verification and commit.

The implementation agent completed cleanly and committed candidate `5a9e63f4ad7090a17847866fae05ac9e0f21e010`; the worktree is clean. Browser-wide tests, formatting, diff checks, and targeted Feature membership/order service tests are green. I’m now following the workflow’s governance/review/merge phases and will verify projection and Git convergence after the candidate leaves the implementation worktree.

A consequential blind spot appeared after implementation: active-queue replenishment has started additional Goals, including a new Goal and a retry of the previously failed bulk-Jira Goal. That behavior matches the recently added replenishment semantics, so the original queue is no longer the whole territory. I’m enumerating the new claims and their live processes before treating the completed candidate’s delayed state transition as a stall.

The first candidate correctly failed governance; I’m not hiding or overriding it. The two concrete findings are: workflow/editability policy was hard-coded in the browser despite broader shared capability semantics, and the “browser coverage” only inspected source text without exercising the DOM interaction/failure paths. Before issuing a retry, I’m checking the already-running singleton supervisor transcript so I don’t duplicate or race its auditable recovery.

The singleton transcript confirms no competing recovery is being issued. I’m using Refine’s own round/state APIs to preserve the failed candidate and create an auditable round focused exactly on the two governance findings; this is a retry, not an override or forced merge.

The failed Feature-authoring Goal is now in round 2 with the governance evidence embedded in its prompt, and the prior worktree/candidate remains intact. Refine also completed the pending Logs review while maintaining six active slots. All six workflow processes currently show forward activity; one is in the full library test gate and the others are editing or mapping their scoped paths.

The queue remains healthy: the autonomous-recovery Goal has produced candidate `ebfeec18…` and is in governance, while the other five active agents continue emitting fresh repository/test activity. No provider/authentication errors or lost claims are present, so I’m leaving healthy work untouched and reserving intervention for explicit governance or execution failures.

The autonomous-recovery candidate also failed governance, with three actionable findings: it encoded recovery judgment as Rust substring heuristics instead of delegating to the supervisor agent; it ignored live workflow claims/capacity; and it could mutate a Goal before required evidence writes, with no end-to-end recovery or write-failure test. I’m preserving its candidate and issuing a second, tightly scoped round that makes recovery agent-first, claim-aware, and evidence-atomic.

Round 2 of inline authoring now passes its new focused shared-capability/API tests. Two Quality-related agents hit non-terminal verification failures (one clippy run and one targeted command chain) and are still active, inspecting/fixing them; these are visible provider/tool outcomes, not being treated as successes. The Jira recovery and supervisor-recovery round 2 also continue with fresh changes.

Three candidates are now under governance (plain-language Quality tests, always-on Quality validation, and the supervisor-recovery candidate’s earlier round history), while the remaining implementation rounds continue. The failed test commands were followed by green focused reruns where applicable; all live process logs have updated within the last minute, so there is still no stall/loss condition.

The plain-language Quality model candidate failed governance on five concrete boundaries: API checks used the primary root instead of the Goal worktree; legacy test commands would silently become a no-op; provider errors/cancellation could leave or overwrite operation state; output validation trusted an `ok` flag without matching per-test evidence; and two affected docs still described removed commands. I’m issuing a preservation round that addresses those exact semantics and migration risks.

The always-on Quality candidate failed for duplicating the Quality capability across workflow/API/settings, for a broken `retry-quality` re-entry path, and for trusting provider self-reported tool strings instead of supervised execution evidence. Because its sibling plain-language Quality core is actively being corrected in round 2 and both touch the same architecture, I’m recording the recovery round now but intentionally not re-queuing it until the shared core lands; that avoids racing two overlapping implementations or forcing a merge.

The inline-authoring round 2 is now committed and under governance. Quality UX has a green full library pass, and supervisor recovery round 2 is in compile/test. The always-on Quality retry remains deliberately parked until the shared core converges; this is an explicit concurrency safeguard, not an abandoned failure.

Inline authoring round 2 cleared the original two findings but failed one narrower governance check: Feature authoring centralized its own create/dedup logic while ordinary Goal creation still retained the old route-local copy. I’m issuing round 3 to make both endpoints call one shared creation/dedup capability and delete the duplicate route helpers.

Quality UX failed for the same architectural split as the always-on Quality candidate, plus silent timing migration. Since both are downstream of the shared Quality core now being corrected, I’m recording its recovery round but parking it too. Re-queuing either downstream Goal before the core lands would repeat the same conflict and waste another agent turn.

The Jira export recovery cleared the original long-running HTTP violation but failed two narrower checks: the Goals UI still lacks visible logs and Cancel/Hide controls, and tests bypass the real managed-process termination/recovery path. I’m issuing round 3 to finish the supervised browser experience and verify real operation-associated process cancellation/interruption.

Four active recovery rounds remain and all are still moving: Jira is adding the production-path operation evidence, inline authoring is compiling the shared create/dedup refactor, supervisor recovery is revising its agent/claim transaction path, and Quality core is implementing migration and supervised evidence handling. No new queue items have been injected.

The four active agents are in focused implementation/test loops with fresh output—no idle process exceeds the stall threshold. The two downstream Quality rounds remain documented but paused, preventing overlapping rewrites while the core migration/evidence contract is still changing.

Inline Feature Goal authoring passed governance in round 3 and reached `review` with candidate `119b532…`; its original implementation, DOM coverage, and shared create/dedup refactor are now all accepted. I’m waiting for normal review completion and will verify the main-branch merge/projection rather than approving by assumption.

Two core candidates failed again, but with narrower, actionable races rather than the original architecture. Supervisor recovery still needs one lock shared with workflow claim mutations, ownership validation before side effects, and a full evidence-plus-decision idempotency fingerprint. Quality core needs API/workflow evidence parity, migration-before-any-settings-write, coupled claim/operation cancellation, and a per-round timing snapshot. I’m issuing one more preservation round for each.

A new Goal arrived through live replenishment: “Keep Supervisor status live during active turns.” I’m adding it to the territory rather than freezing the initial queue snapshot. The earlier inline-authoring Goal remains safely in review; review is not being auto-approved across a confirmation boundary merely to make counts look clean.

The new live-status Goal is correctly reusing supervisor reconciliation/chat/SSE rather than adding another monitor. The three recovery rounds are still active with fresh output; Jira’s agent also ran a read-only governance precheck inside its worktree before continuing, which is why that turn is longer, but the provider completed that nested check rather than hanging.

I’m resuming from the live workflow boundary: four implementation rounds were active, one shared-core Quality follow-up was deliberately staged but not requeued, and its UX successor remains parked behind it. I’ll reconcile process files against Goal state first, then advance only the dependency-safe recoveries.

All four rounds are still live and emitting evidence. The Jira recovery has completed 399 Rust and 23 browser tests and is in governance re-audit; the live-status fix passed its focused backend/browser regressions and is running the broader gate. I’m leaving those healthy processes undisturbed and checking the two shared-state rounds for convergence or a fresh audit finding.
